coredata.dc:title: A dual-mode sensing system based on carbon quantum dots and Fe nanozymes for the detection of α-glucosidase and its inhibitors
coredata.dc:creator: Lv, Yuntai
Zhou, Chenyu
Li, Meini
Huo, Zejiao
Wei, Zhiyuan
Wang, Nan
Wang, Guannan
Su, Xingguang
coredata.dc:description: In this research, a sensitive fluorometric and colorimetric dual-mode sensing platform based on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs) and magnetic Fe nanoparticles with peroxidase-like activity (Fe nanozymes, Fe NZs) was established, and was further applied for the detection of α-glucosidase (α-glu) and its inhibitors. The ⋅OH that produced by H2O2 catalyzed by Fe NZs can oxidize the colorless diammonium 2,2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonate) (ABTS) to green oxABTS, and a noticeable absorption peak at 417 nm appeared. Simultaneously, oxABTS can quench the fluorescence of NCDs at 402 nm via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). 2-O-α-D-glucopyranosyl-L-ascorbic acid (AAG) can be decomposed by α-glu to glucose and ascorbic acid (AA), AA can prevent the oxidation of ABTS, resulting in the absorption at 417 nm decreased. Moreover, the quenching effect of oxABTS on NCDs is weakened, and the fluorescence at 402 nm is restored. Therefore, based on the change of absorption at 417 nm and fluorescence at 402 nm, the fluorometric and colorimetric dual-mode sensing method can be used for the determination of acarbose and voglibose that are the inhibitors of α-glu.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Colorimetric fluorometric dual-mode
Nanozyme
α-glucosidase
Acarbose
Voglibose

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of NCDs and Fe NZs
Steady-state kinetic analysis of Fe NZs
Detection of α-glu and its inhibitors
Detection of α-glu in human serum
Characterization of NCDs and Fe NZs
Exploration of peroxidase-like properties of Fe NZs
Quantitative detection of acarbose and voglibose
Analysis of α-glu in human serum samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21775052 ), the Science and Technology Development project of Jilin province, China (No. 20220402001GH ), the Open Funds of the State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, China (No. SKLEAC202201 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (A), fluorescence excitation, emission and UV–Vis absorption spectra (B) of NCDs. Fluorescence emission spectra at different excitation wavelength (C) and FT-IR spectrum (D) of NCDs. XPS survey spectra (E) and XPS spectra for C 1s (F) of NCDs.

(A) TEM image (Inset: HRTEM image) and (B) XRD image of Fe NZs. (C) UV–Vis absorption spectra of ABTS, TMB, OPD and DPD with Fe NZs catalytic system and their photograph (inset of C). (D) UV–Vis absorption spectra of ABTS + H2O2, NCDs + ABTS + H2O2 and Fe NZs + ABTS + H2O2. (E) Fluorescence emission spectra of TA + H2O2 and TA + H2O2+Fe NZs. (F) Absorbance of Fe NZs catalytic system with different concentration of IPA.
(A) UV–Vis spectrum of oxABTS and fluorescence emission spectrum of NCDs. Zeta potentials (B), fluorescence decay curves (C) and UV–Vis spectrum (D) of NCDs, oxABTS and oxABTS + NCDs.

UV–Vis absorption (A) and fluorescence emission spectra (B) of NCDs with various substances. UV–Vis absorption (C) and fluorescence emission spectra (D) of Fe NZs/ABTS/H2O2/NCDs system (Blank) with AAG, α-glu and α-glu + AAG.

(A) Absorption spectra of Fe NZs/ABTS/H2O2/NCDs/AAG system with various α-glu activities. (B) Fitting curve of α-glu activities versus the ΔA. (C) Fluorescence spectra of Fe NZs/ABTS/H2O2/NCDs/AAG system with various α-glu activities. (D) Fitting curve of α-glu activities versus the F/F0. Inhibition ratio of inhibitors and linear plot of inhibition ratio between logarithmic value of inhibitors concentration (inset of E, F, G and H) in colorimetric (E: acarbose and G: voglibose) and fluorometric (F: acarbose and H: voglibose) mode.

Selectivity (green columns) and anti-interference (orange columns) analysis for α-glu activities in colorimetric (A) and fluorometric (B) mode. The concentration of inorganic ions, biomolecules and amino acids was 1 mM and activity of enzymes was 100 U/L.
Illustration of utilizing Fe NZs and NCDs for α-glu activity and its inhibitors detection.
Results of α-glu analysis in human serum samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A dual-mode sensing system based on carbon quantum dots and Fe nanozymes for the detection of α-glucosidase and its inhibitors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Medical University, Shenyang,110034, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Medical Engineering & the Key Laboratory for Medical Functional Nanomaterials, Jining Medical University, Jining 272067, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this research, a sensitive fluorometric and colorimetric dual-mode sensing platform based on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs) and magnetic Fe nanoparticles with peroxidase-like activity (Fe nanozymes, Fe NZs) was established, and was further applied for the detection of α-glucosidase (α-glu) and its inhibitors. The ⋅OH that produced by H2O2 catalyzed by Fe NZs can oxidize the colorless diammonium 2,2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonate) (ABTS) to green oxABTS, and a noticeable absorption peak at 417 nm appeared. Simultaneously, oxABTS can quench the fluorescence of NCDs at 402 nm via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). 2-O-α-D-glucopyranosyl-L-ascorbic acid (AAG) can be decomposed by α-glu to glucose and ascorbic acid (AA), AA can prevent the oxidation of ABTS, resulting in the absorption at 417 nm decreased. Moreover, the quenching effect of oxABTS on NCDs is weakened, and the fluorescence at 402 nm is restored. Therefore, based on the change of absorption at 417 nm and fluorescence at 402 nm, the fluorometric and colorimetric dual-mode sensing method can be used for the determination of acarbose and voglibose that are the inhibitors of α-glu.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: α-glucosidase (α-glu) has the ability to catalyze the hydrolysis of α-1,4-glucoside [1]. Research reports have shown that activity of α-glu in semen is correlated with sperm vitality and number. If the activity of α-glu in semen is too low, it may lead to infertility in men [2,3]. In addition to semen, α-glu is also found in the epithelial chorionic membrane of the small intestine, and its activity is closely related to blood glucose concentration [4]. People with type II diabetes need to keep their blood glucose levels within the normal range, and one way they do that is by taking α-glucosidase inhibitors (AGIs) [5,6]. In addition to miglitol and voglibose, acarbose is also a kind of common AGIs [7]. Because AGIs can compete with oligosaccharides, so the process of oligosaccharides being decomposed into monosaccharides by α-glu is inhibited, slowing down the body's absorption of glucose and thus controlling blood glucose. Therefore, accurate quantitative analysis and detection of α-glu and its inhibitors have important biochemical and medical significance.

The common sensing methods of α-glu include fluorimetry [8], colorimetry [9], electrochemistry [10] and so on. Liu et al. used silicon quantum dots and MnO2 nanosheets to construct a fluorometric sensing platform to detect α-glu and acarbose [11]. Kang et al. fabricated Pt-SAzymes and used it for colorimetric determination of α-glu activity [12]. However, the above sensing platforms for α-glu activity detection only output a single signal, which is easy to be interfered and reduces the accuracy of the results. Therefore, a sensing platform capable of output multiple signals is desired for the detection of the activity of α-glu. Colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode detection method output two different optical signals for self-correction and self-verification which provide more reliable detection results [13]. Liu et al. detected fenitrothion by colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode method [14]. Jia et al. used CDs to detected glutathione by colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode method [15].

Nanozyme is a class of nanomaterials with enzyme-like activity [16,17]. Compared with natural enzyme, nanozyme has many advantages, such as low price, stable property, convenient storage and adjustable activity [18,19]. Among them, peroxide-like nanozyme are more broadly used to build sensing platform for biochemical analysis [20,21]. Zhang et al. designed a multienzyme cascades colorimetric sensing platform with Fe–N–C for the detection of α-glu activity [22]. Xue et al. designed MoO2@NP-doped carbon nano-octahedron to construct a colorimetric sensing platform to detect alkaline phosphatase activity [23].

Carbon quantum dots (CDs) have been utilized frequently as fluorescent probes for the identification of biomolecules because of their good biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity and high photobleaching resistance [24–26]. Yin et al. used BNSCDs and V2O5 nanoribbons to establish an off-on fluorometric sensing platform to detect α-glu activity [27]. Hu et al. assembled CDs into ZIF-8 to form CDs@ZIF-8 for detection of activity of butyrylcholinesterase [28].

In this work, we have developed a sensing platform utilizing magnetic Fe nanozymes (Fe NZs) and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs) for fluorometric and colorimetric detection of α-glu and acarbose. As shown in Scheme 1 , H2O2 is decomposed under the catalysis of Fe NZs to generate ⋅OH which can oxidize colorless diammonium 2,2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonate) (ABTS) to green oxABTS with a distinct peak at 417 nm for absorption. Simultaneously, NCDs fluorescence at 402 nm is quenched by oxABTS via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). 2-O-α-D-glucopyranosyl-L-ascorbic acid (AAG) can be decomposed by α-glu to produce glucose and ascorbic acid (AA), AA can impede the conversion of the colorless ABTS to green oxABTS, leading the absorption at 417 nm decrease. Because oxABTS quenching effect on NCDs fluorescence is weakened, the fluorescence at 402 nm is restored. Thus, we have developed a colorimetric fluorometric dual-signal sensing platform for detection of α-glu activity as well as acarbose and voglibose.
Related materials and instrumentations were provided in supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of NCDs and Fe NZs
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of NCDs was slightly improved on the basis of previously reported method [29]. After 0.1 g o-phenylenediamine was dissolved in 5 mL ultra-pure water, prepared o-phenylenediamine solution was mixed with 25 mL glyoxal (40 wt%) and stirred for 3 h 1 M NaOH was added to the mixed solution until no precipitation was generated, and precipitation was obtained by centrifugation at 12000 rpm for 10 min. Then cleaned precipitation with ultra-pure water three times, and finally dissolved precipitation with 0.1 M HCl to obtain NCDs solution.

The preparation of Fe NZs was according to the previous report with a slight improvement [30]. NaBH4 (75 mM, 100 mL) was added to the FeCl3 (25 mM, 100 mL) by drops for 60 min. To obtain the final product, the resulting black powder was washed three times in alternating cycles with ultra-pure water and ethanol before being dried overnight at 70 °C in a vacuum oven.

The radical scavenging experiment as follows: H2O2 (10 mM, 100 μL), HAc-NaAc buffer (0.1 M, pH 3.5, 300 μL), ABTS (10 mM, 10 μL), Fe NZs (1 g/L, 10 μL) and different volumes of isopropyl alcohol (IPA) were mixed, then mixed the solution with ultra-pure water until the volume was 1 mL. The solution was then treated at 40 °C for 2 min. Thereafter, the absorbance spectra were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Steady-state kinetic analysis of Fe NZs
ce:section.ce:para: H2O2 and ABTS were used as substrates respectively to analyze steady-state kinetic of Fe NZs. With H2O2 as substrate, the concentration range of H2O2 was 0.1–3.0 mM, while the concentration of ABTS was fixed at 0.4 mM. With ABTS as substrate, the concentration range of ABTS was 0.1–3.0 mM, while the concentration of H2O2 was fixed at 1 mM. Absorbance of solution at 417 nm was measured every 30 s until 180 s. The steady state dynamic parameters of Fe NZs can be obtained from the Michaelis-Menten equation in double reciprocal form: 1/v0=(Km/Vmax) × (1/[S])+1/Vmax. In the formula, v0 is initial reaction velocity, Vmax is the maximal reaction velocity, [S] is the concentration of the substrate and Km is the Michaelis-Menten constant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of α-glu and its inhibitors
ce:section.ce:para: The α-glu was determined as follows: Different volumes of α-glu (1000 U/L) were mixed with AAG (100 mM, 70 μL) and Tris-HCl buffer (0.1 M, pH 7.0, 40 μL) to react at 40 °C for 30 min. Then, H2O2 (10 mM, 100 μL), HAc-NaAc buffer (0.1 M, pH 3.5, 300 μL), ABTS (10 mM, 10 μL), Fe NZs (1 g/L, 10 μL) were added to the solution and mixed with ultra-pure water until the volume was 1 mL. The solution was then treated at 40 °C for 2 min. Thereafter, the absorbance and fluorescence spectra were respectively recorded.

The determination method of acarbose or voglibose was similar to the method for α-glu, the difference was that α-glu (1000 U/L, 100 μL) was mixed with different volumes of acarbose or voglibose (0.1 mM). Other steps were consistent with the determination for α-glu.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of α-glu in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: The healthy human serum samples were obtained from Changchun China Japan United Hospital. Informed consent was obtained from the human participants of this study. Before detection, the serum samples were diluted ten times and incubated with N-ethylmaleimide (NEM). The human serum samples were mixed with different volumes of α-glu (1000 U/L) to obtain spiked human serum samples. The method for measuring α-glu in human serum samples was similar to the described in section 2.5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of NCDs and Fe NZs
ce:section.ce:para: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image showed that near-spherical NCDs had good dispersion (Fig. 1 A). The average particle size of NCDs was 7.73 nm. Maximum excitation and emission wavelength of NCDs was 270 and 402 nm. NCDs had absorption peaks at 245 and 275 nm due to π-π* and n-π* electron transition [31] (Fig. 1B). Under different excitation wavelength, the fluorescence emission peak of NCDs changed obviously that showed excitation-dependent emission characteristic (Fig. 1C). Surface functional groups of NCDs was explored by FT-IR spectrum. Fig. 1D exhibited the stretching vibrations of the chemical bonds of NCDs corresponding to N–H or O–H (3418 cm−1), C=N or C=O (1654 cm−1), -COO- (1384 cm−1) and C–O (1087 cm−1) [32]. N–H, O–H and -COO- indicated that the surface of NCDs had amine, hydroxyl and carboxylic groups, those hydrophilic functional groups endowed good water solubility of NCDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were subsequently used to analyze elements of NCDs. C, N and O in NCDs could be seen in XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 1E). C 1s had four characteristic peaks of C–O (284.40 eV), C–C/C=C (285.10 eV), C=C–N (286.10 eV) and C=N (287.10 eV) in XPS high-resolution spectra [33] (Fig. 1F). N 1s showed four characteristic peaks of N–H (401.20 eV), N-(C)3 (400.60 eV), C–N–C (399.95 eV) and C=N (398.60 eV) [34] (Fig. S1A). O=C–O* (532.90 eV), *O=C–O (532.15 eV) and –OH (531.45 eV) were the three characteristic peaks of O 1s [35] (Fig. S1B). The above XPS high-resolution spectra once again proved that NCDs had hydrophilic functional groups. We investigated impact of pH on NCDs fluorescence, and discovered that fluorescence intensity of NCDs was stronger in acidic environment, fluorescence intensity of NCDs steadily reduced from pH 3.0 to 7.0, and kept unchangeable with further increase of pH to 9.0. it was ascribed to deprotonation and protonation of surface functional groups [36] (Fig. S2A). The fluorescence intensity of NCDs was almost the same as initial after 30 days storage at room temperature, showing good stability (Fig. S2B). When xenon lamp was used to irradiate NCDs for 1 h, the fluorescence intensity of NCDs did not have obvious decrease, which showed that NCDs had excellent photobleaching resistance (Fig. S2C).

Fig. S3 showed that Fe NZs had good dispersion in solution and could be separated from the solution under magnetic field, implying that Fe NZs were magnetic, and could be separated and recycled in solution. The TEM image of Fe NZs revealed that Fe NZs was granular, and because of the strong magnetism, Fe NZs clustered to chain structure (Fig. 2 A). The HRTEM image showed that surface of Fe NZs had shell structure (inset of Fig. 2A). The XRD pattern of Fe NZs showed that the peaks of Fe NZs were exactly consistent with those of the standard iron sample (PDF 06–0696) (Fig. 2B). The above characterization proved the successful synthesis of Fe NZs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploration of peroxidase-like properties of Fe NZs
ce:section.ce:para: ABTS, 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and N,N-diethyl-p-phenylenediamine sulfate salt (DPD) were used as chromogenic substrates to evaluate the peroxide-like activity of nanozyme. Four chromogenic substrates, ABTS, TMB, OPD and DPD, were added into the Fe NZs/H2O2 system respectively, which made the originally colorless solution turned into green, blue, yellow and pink, and had obvious absorption peaks at 417, 653, 449 and 551 nm respectively (Fig. 2C). Above results indicated that Fe NZs had peroxide-like activity. Among the above four chromogenic substrates, ABTS was selected as the chromogenic substrate in this experiment. Because the absorption peak of oxABTS overlapped with the fluorescence emission peak of NCD which provided the prerequisite for quenching the fluorescence of NCDs. When NCDs and Fe NZs were added to the H2O2/ABTS system, only the H2O2/ABTS system with Fe NZs had a characteristic absorption peak at 417 nm. However, this peak did not appear in either the H2O2/ABTS system or the H2O2/ABTS/NCDs system (Fig. 2D), which indicated that the only source of catalytic activity for the sensing platform were Fe NZs rather than NCDs. Non-fluorescent terephthalic acid (TA) would react with the ⋅OH [37], and the maximum emission wavelength of the fluorescent product was located at 426 nm. When only TA and H2O2 existed in the solution, no fluorescence peak was generated at 426 nm. However, when Fe NZs were added to TA/H2O2 system, an apparent fluorescence peak was shown at 426 nm (Fig. 2E), which indicated that Fe NZs could catalytic decompose H2O2 into ⋅OH and ⋅OH reacted with TA to produce blue fluorescent products. Furthermore, isopropyl alcohol (IPA) was served as the scavenger of ⋅OH [38], the addition of IPA to Fe NZs/H2O2/ABTS system reduced absorbance of oxABTS at 417 nm, and the higher the concentration of IPA added to system, the greater the reduction of oxABTS absorbance at 417 nm (Fig. 2F), which indicated that the ⋅OH produced by H2O2 under the catalytic action of Fe NZs can transformed ABTS into oxABTS. The reusability experiment proved that Fe NZs still retained good activity after four repeated measurements, indicating the acceptable reusability (Fig. S4).

The peroxidase activity of Fe NZs was studied by a steady-state kinetic experiment. Under optimal experimental conditions, Michaelis-Menten curves of ABTS and H2O2 were obtained respectively (Figs. S5A and C), and their Lineweaver-Burk plots were obtained after double-reciprocal processing (Figs. S5B and D). Finally, the Km and Vmax of Fe NZs with respect to ABTS and H2O2 were obtained. Table S1 showed that Km of Fe NZs for substrate ABTS and H2O2 were 0.49 mM and 0.12 mM respectively, which were lower than many mimicking peroxidase materials and horseradish peroxidase (HRP). This indicated that Fe NZs had a better affinity for ABTS and H2O2, and that Fe NZs had high efficiency peroxidase-like activity.

Absorption spectrum of oxABTS and fluorescence emission spectrum of NCDs were measured to explore mechanism of oxABTS quenching NCDs fluorescence (Fig. 3 A). Fluorescence emission peak of NCD and absorption peak of oxABTS overlapped, suggesting that IFE or FRET could be the reasons for oxABTS quenching the fluorescence of NCDs [39]. To clarify whether there was IFE or FRET between NCDs and oxABTS, we investigated the zeta potential and the fluorescence lifetime. The zeta potential of oxABTS and NCDs was −9.16 mV and +33.15 mV respectively, while the zeta potential of oxABTS mixed with NCDs was +24.10 mV (Fig. 3B). This indicated that oxABTS can adsorb on NCDs surface by electrostatic interaction, making distance between oxABTS and NCDs narrow, providing the condition for the occurrence of FRET [40]. The fluorescence lifetime of NCDs was 5.11 ns, when oxABTS was added to NCDs, the fluorescence lifetime of NCDs was reduced to 3.00 ns (Fig. 3C), which suggested that FRET was involved in the fluorescence quenching mechanism of NCDs by oxABTS. Fig. 3D shown that the absorption spectra of oxABTS did not change significantly in the presence of NCDs, indicating that the NCDs had slight impact on oxABTS absorbance, which would not affect the accuracy of the colorimetric sensing platform.

Under the catalysis of Fe NZs, H2O2 was decomposed into ⋅OH. The ⋅OH oxidized the colorless ABTS into green oxABTS, and distinctive absorption peak was visible at 417 nm. Meanwhile, oxABTS suppressed fluorescence emission of NCDs at 402 nm. However, when AA was added to the Fe NZs/H2O2/ABTS/NCDs system, AA would prevent the oxidation of colorless ABTS to green oxABTS, and would reduce absorbance of system at 417 nm, which weakened quenching effect of oxABTS on NCDs, and thus restored the fluorescence intensity of NCDs (Fig. 4 A and B). AAG can be hydrolyzed by α-glu to produce glucose and AA. Therefore, α-glu could be detected via fluorometric and colorimetric dual-mode sensing. Fig. 4C and D revealed that when AAG and α-glu were added separately to Fe NZs/ABTS/H2O2/NCDs system, the fluorescence and absorbance of the system did not change. All these results indicated that the fluorometric colorimetric sensing platform based on Fe NZs/H2O2/ABTS/NCDs was feasible for the detection of α-glu.

To obtain best peroxide-like activity of Fe NZs, pH value, temperature and reaction time of the Fe NZs/ABTS/H2O2 system were optimized. The catalytic activity of Fe NZs reached the best when the reaction pH value was 3.5, and catalytic activity decreased obviously as the increase of the reaction pH value (Fig. S6A). This could be due to the acidic condition contributed to the formation of ⋅OH [30]. The catalytic activity of Fe NZs improved with an increasing in temperature and had the optimal value at 40 °C. However, when the reaction temperature exceeded 40 °C, the catalytic activity of Fe NZs began to decline with the reaction temperature increased further (Fig. S6B). Because as the reaction temperature increased, the activity of Fe NPs also increased, but too high temperature was not conducive to the reaction [30]. Fig. S6C shown that the absorbance of Fe NZs/ABTS/H2O2 system at 417 nm increased with the increase of reaction time, but hardly increased after 2 min. So, reaction temperature of 40 °C, pH value of 3.5 and reaction time of 2 min were chosen for further experiments.

The reaction conditions for α-glu detection were further optimized. Fig. S7A shown the Fe NZs/ABTS/H2O2/NCDs/AAG/α-glu system had a minimal absorbance at 417 nm at the reaction temperature of 40 °C, indicating the amount of AA produced by the hydrolysis of AAG by α-glu was the highest. Similarly, the optimal pH for α-glu reaction was pH 7.0 (Fig. S7B). With the increase of AAG concentration, the absorbance of system at 417 nm also decreased. When the concentration of AAG was 7 mM, the absorbance was almost no longer decreased even the concentration of AAG was further increased (Fig. S7C). When the reaction time of AAG and α-glu was 30 min, the absorbance at 417 nm reached minimum and remained constant with the increase of time (Fig. S7D). So, reaction temperature of 40 °C, pH value of 7.0, reaction time of 30 min and AAG concentration of 7 mM were the optimal conditions for detection of α-glu.

Under the optimal reaction conditions, fluorometric and colorimetric were utilized to quantitatively measure the activity of α-glu. As the increase of α-glu activity, the absorbance value of Fe NZs/ABTS/H2O2/NCDs/AAG system at 417 nm decreased gradually. When the activity of α-glu was among 5 and 100 U/L, there was an obvious linear relationship between α-glu activity and the value of absorbance, which could be expressed by the linear equation ΔA = 1.3322–0.0119[α-glu] (R2 = 0.994) and the LOD of α-glu was 2.1 U/L (based on 3σ/k rule) (Fig. 5 A and B). Where ΔA = A-A0, A0 and A represented the absorbance of system before and after the addition of α-glu. With the increase of α-glu activity, the fluorescence signal of the Fe NZs/ABTS/H2O2/NCDs/AAG system at 402 nm increased gradually. When the activity of α-glu was between 5 U/L and 100 U/L, there was a significant linear relationship between α-glu activity and fluorescence intensity, which could be expressed by the linear equation F/F0 = 0.0064 + 0.1843[α-glu] (R2 = 0.996) and the LOD of α-glu was 2.4 U/L (Fig. 5C and D). Where F0 and F were the fluorescence intensity of system before and after the addition of α-glu. The relative standard deviations of α-glu colorimetric and fluorometric detection at different concentrations in three replicates were below 4.95 % and 9.11 %, which indicated the good reproducibility of our methods. Table S2 listed some assays used to detect α-glu activity. Compared with previously reported assays, our method had lower detection limit. In addition, this work was a dual-mode detection method, which provided two different optical signals for self-correction and self-verification, so the results provided were more accurate and reliable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative detection of acarbose and voglibose
ce:section.ce:para: As acarbose and voglibose could inhibit catalytic activity of α-glu, so it was possible to quantitatively detect them based on the degree of reduction in α-glu activity. Fig. 5 showed that the inhibition ratio (I, %) of inhibitors increased with an increase of inhibitors concentration (Fig. 5E, F, G and H), where I represented the ratio of the activity of α-glu with and without inhibitors. When the inhibitors concentration was between 0.1 μM and 10 μM, there was a significant linear relationship between value of inhibition ratio and logarithmic value of inhibitors concentration at both colorimetric and fluorometric detection (inset of Fig. 5E, F, G and H). The IC50 values of acarbose were 0.825 μM (in colorimetry) and 0.880 μM (in fluorimetry), and IC50 values of voglibose were 1.33 μM (in colorimetry) and 1.39 μM (in fluorimetry). These results indicated that the fluorometric colorimetric dual-mode sensor platform used for the detection of α-glu as well as for acarbose and voglibose.

In addition to sensitivity, selectivity and anti-interference were also important criteria to measure whether a sensing system was excellent. To investigate whether the fluorometric colorimetric dual-mode sensing platform also had excellent selectivity and anti-interference for detection of α-glu, several inorganic ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Cl−, SO4 2−), amino acids (cysteine (Cys), alanine (Ala), glycine (Gly), arginine (Arg), tryptophan (Trp)), biomolecules (AA, uric acid (UA), glutathione (GSH), glucose, sucrose, maltose) and enzymes (glucose oxidase (GOx), acid phosphatase (ACP), alkaline phosphatase (ALP), trypsin, pepsin, β-galactosidase (β-gal)) were selected as interfering substances. The substance (AA, UA, GSH, glucose, sucrose, maltose) shown in Fig. 6 had an influence on the test results. However, in the actual sample test, their content in the serum treated with NEM was much lower than the amount used in the interference experiment, so their interference effect on the serum test results was negligible. In addition to the above-mentioned interfering substances, Fig. 6 shown that only α-glu could decrease ΔA or increase F/F0, while other interfering substances had no significant effect on ΔA and F/F0 and when other interfering substances coexisted with α-glu, ΔA and F/F0 were similar with that when only α-glu was present. According to the above results, this fluorometric and colorimetric dual-mode sensing platform had excellent selectivity and anti-interference for detection of α-glu.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of α-glu in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the ability of this fluorometric colorimetric dual-mode sensing platform to detect α-glu in real sample, human serum was selected to be the actual sample. Table 1 listed recovery and relative standard deviation (RSD) of colorimetric and fluorometric determination of α-glu in human serum, respectively. Recovery ranged from 94.24 % to 103.69 % with RSD less than 8.01 % for fluorometric assay. Recovery ranged from 96.10 % to 102.58 % with the RSD less than 6.70 % for colorimetric assay. The α-glu could hydrolyze 4-nitrophenyl-α-D-glucopyranoside (PNPG) to p-nitrophenol and the concentration of p-nitrophenol was linearly correlated with the activity of α-glu, so the PNPG assay can be used as a control experiment of our detection method (Fig. S8)[41]. As shown in Table 1, the results obtained by our work were consistent with those of the control assay based on PNPG. These indicated the fluorometric colorimetric dual-mode sensing platform established in this work could be used for detection of α-glu in human serum with good precision and accuracy.

In summary, we constructed a colorimetric and fluorometric dual-mode sensing system that utilized NCDs and Fe NZs. This sensing system could be applied to detect α-glu and acarbose sensitively based on the change of absorption at 417 nm and fluorescence at 402 nm. The established dual-mode sensing platform has been successful applied in the α-glu assay in real samples. Compared with α-glu sensors that could only output a single signal, our proposed sensor platform had the characteristics of dual signal output, enabling it to self-correct and self-validate, and provided more reliable detection results.

Yuntai Lv: Conceptualization, Validation, Investigation, Software, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Chenyu Zhou: Formal analysis, Data curation. Meini Li: Data curation. Zejiao Huo: Software, Visualization. Zhiyuan Wei: Software. Nan Wang: Investigation. Guannan Wang: Project administration. Xingguang Su: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21775052), the Science and Technology Development project of Jilin province, China (No. 20220402001GH), the Open Funds of the State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, China (No. SKLEAC202201).
